topic: Two strangers realize they've been dating the same person
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee in my hand suddenly felt like acid. I watched him across the crowded gallery, the way he tilted his head when the curator spoke, the slight, almost imperceptible crinkle at the corner of his eyes. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. It was impossible. He was a ghost, a fleeting acquaintance from a mutual friend’s party. Yet, there was a familiarity in the cadence of his laugh, a specific way he held his shoulders that snagged something deep within me. When he finally turned, catching my gaze, a flicker of something—recognition mixed with a profound sense of annoyance—crossed his face before he offered a polite, utterly blank smile. I took a deliberate sip of my drink, my jaw setting tight. This was ridiculous. I felt a sharp urge to simply walk away, to erase this unsettling overlap of reality.

[story 2]

"Excuse me, is this seat taken?" The question was automatic, a practiced deflection. I glanced up, and there he was. The same slightly rumpled linen shirt, the familiar, infuriatingly easy way he leaned against the doorframe. My breath hitched, a tiny, involuntary spasm in my chest. It was the same shade of tired blue in his eyes, the exact way his dark hair fell across his forehead. A wave of irritation washed over me, hot and prickly. I forced myself to meet his gaze, my expression carefully neutral, a mask of polite indifference. He offered a small, knowing smirk, a gesture that felt deeply condescending. I shifted in my seat, deliberately angling my body away, trying to create a physical barrier against the unwelcome familiarity radiating off him.

[story 3]

I was waiting for the train, the platform echoing with the mundane sounds of commuters. Then I saw him. Standing by the departure board, adjusting the strap of his worn messenger bag, the familiar slump of his posture immediately setting off a low, simmering resentment. It was a ridiculous coincidence, I thought, a cruel joke played by fate. But the way he moved, the unconscious habit of brushing a stray piece of lint from his jacket—it was all too precise. When our eyes met, I felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a sharp refusal to acknowledge the connection. I kept my gaze fixed on the tracks ahead, focusing intently on the blurring lights, wishing desperately for the moment to dissolve, to simply cease existing in my periphery.
